pages.name: Ostren Pell — The Leak in the Office

text.content:
Ostren Pell

The leak in Caelith's office. Not a mastermind. Not actively malicious. Recruited through ambition (a gambling debt his late father left him, paid off two years ago by an outside party); kept in place by fear (his unmarried older sister Sera, named in the second envelope). He is drowning. He believes he has no exit.

Description

Early thirties. Loftwick-born, the son of a stationer who died of consumption when Ostren was nineteen. Thin build, prematurely receding hair worn long at the back to compensate, ink-stained fingertips. Wears a respectable but worn brown clerk's coat with a small enameled Audit Hall pin (still — the pin has not been recalled). His coat collar is always slightly higher than the next person's, as if to give him a thin further layer of privacy. Speaks softly. Apologizes more than he should.

Background

Apprenticed at the Audit Hall at sixteen on his late father's modest standing. Promoted to the correspondence office at twenty-six. Assigned to Caelith's small staff at twenty-nine. He has been Caelith's correspondence clerk for four years and has been leaking summaries for two of those four. Lives in two rooms above his unmarried older sister Sera Pell's modest dressmaker's shop on Wick Lane (the same lane where Merro Gilst — the Phase 2 dead bonds clerk — kept his rented room above the stationer's). Sera raised him after their parents died. She is forty-one, sharp-eyed, and unaware of any of this.

Why he leaks

The hook was the gambling debt his father had left him. Three thousand gp owed to a Loftwick discount house, with interest accruing. An outside party (Pell does not know the name; the contact called himself only "the man on the lane") approached Pell at twenty-eight and paid the debt in full in exchange for a single small favor: forwarding a sanitized monthly summary of Caelith's foreign correspondence. Pell agreed because he was twenty-eight and very tired and Sera had been hoarding her dressmaker's profits to help him. He did it once. He told himself it would be only once.

The lock arrived four months later in the form of a second envelope with no message in it — only Sera's name written in the same hand as the contact's first envelope. Pell understood. He has been forwarding sanitized summaries every nine to fourteen days for the twenty months since. He has tried, twice, to find a way out. The first time he stopped sending; an envelope arrived with Sera's address written below her name. The second time he tried to confess to Caelith and lost his nerve at the office door.

What he knows

  • The dead-drop location: a hollowed-out brick in the back wall of a small civic copy room two streets from the Audit Hall, behind a ledger-binder's annex. The brick is third from the bottom, fourth from the left, in a wall that no one has recoursed in fifteen years.
  • The courier signal: a cold-stove rag (specifically — a grey woolen cloth, not a scrap) hung over the back step of his sister's shop on the morning of pickup. The signal goes up before dawn; the courier walks past at the seventh bell; the rag is gone by the eighth.
  • One face: the courier himself. Pell does not know the courier's name. Pell has seen him three times — twice across the cooper's lane, once at the civic courier desk where Pell was filing routine post. Mid-thirties, brown beard, plain dark cloak, walks with a slight forward lean. (See the Courier Chain page in Phase 5 — Leak Operation.)

What he does not know

  • The handler's name. (The handler is Vesten Quill, but Pell has never heard the name and has never seen Quill.)
  • What use his summaries are put to.
  • Whether Sera is being watched. (She is — passively. The handler has not approached her or her shop since the second envelope.)

Voice

Soft, deferential, apologetic. Uses the formal Yeomanry plural in writing ("we have noted," "we are forwarding") but the bare singular when he is afraid ("I am sorry. I do not know."). When confronted, his first instinct is to confess everything in one continuous breath; his second instinct, three sentences in, is to begin negotiating for Sera. Both are real. Let players decide which they trust.

What Caelith wants the party to do with him

Caelith will not direct the party. He will lay out the five choices (see The Leak — Five Choices in Phase 5 — Leak Operation), describe the consequences of each, and abide by the party's decision. He will privately favor either Turn Pell or Feed Pell False Information — both keep the channel open and protect Sera. He will accept Expose Pell Publicly if the party insists, but he will lose the channel and Sera will be safer. He will refuse to support Remove Pell from the Office unless paired with extraction of Sera. He will refuse to accept Killing Pell except in extreme circumstances; the moral cost lands at the table because Pell is, at this point, a man cornered by his own one bad decision and his sister's name.

Statblock summary (CR 0, PB +2)

AC 10 · HP 9 (2d8) · Speed 30 ft. · Init +0 · Passive Perception 11

STR 9 (-1) · DEX 11 (+0) · CON 10 (+0) · INT 14 (+2) · WIS 12 (+1) · CHA 10 (+0)

Skills: Deception +2, Insight +3, Investigation +4, Sleight of Hand +2.

Senses: Passive Perception 11. Languages: Common, Old Aerdi (reads only), Yeomanry diplomatic cipher (writes only the simplified form).

Combat behavior: none. Yields immediately. Will physically resist only if the party attempts to harm Sera in front of him; even then he will not attack — he will throw himself in front of her.

pages.name: Mistress Aldea Veren — Trina's Old Correspondent in the Wood

text.content:
Mistress Aldea Veren

Trina's old correspondent in the wood. Half-elven retired scholar of pre-Aerdy paleography and sealing materials. Lives alone in a small stone cottage in a wooded valley a day's careful ride west of Loftwick, in the foothills of the Stark Mounds. She will not travel to Loftwick. The party may visit her in person to ask the questions her written reply could not safely answer.

Description

Looks late forties. Is older — exactly how much older Trina has never asked and Aldea has never volunteered. Half-elven, fair-skinned with a faint olive cast, dark-hazel hair worn loose to the shoulders and lightly streaked with silver. Slow-moving but not infirm; precise in her hands. Plain grey wool dress; a heavy leather apron worn for workshop time and removed for visitors. Reading lenses on a fine cord around her neck. A small dark silver ring on her left hand bearing a worn device that may once have been a scroll-and-quill (it is too worn to be sure).

Background

Practiced as a paleographer and sealing-materials specialist for nearly seventy years across the southern Sheldomar — Loftwick's Audit Hall briefly, the Keoish trade-clearing courts longer, a Suel-aligned scriptorium in the deep south for the better part of two decades. Retired in the late 1560s and bought the small holding she now lives on, three days' careful ride west and slightly south of Loftwick. Has corresponded with Trina Alvere by trusted courier for nearly thirty years. She and Trina have met in person twice in those three decades. They are not friends in any conventional sense. They are correspondents.

The valley

A small stone cottage in a sheltered fold of the Stark Mounds foothills, surrounded by scrubby beech and silver birch. A workshop adjoins the cottage on the south side, with a door wide enough to take in furniture or a stack of folio volumes. A small herb garden behind the cottage; a slow stream below it, running clear. The valley is not patrolled but it is observed (by Aldea, by the things that live there). Animals — a brindle dog, a half-tame fox, two goats, a great horned owl on the workshop roof — are unbothered by her presence and unbothered by the presence of strangers she has chosen to receive. Strangers she has not chosen to receive find the path harder to follow.

This is the "fey-tinge" the GM should let through. It is environmental. Never name it. Never let Aldea explain it. The animals are not familiars. The stove that stays warm in her kitchen does not stay warm because of magic she casts; it stays warm because a small green-and-grey thing the size of a hand sleeps under the lintel of the chimney, and Aldea has known the small green-and-grey thing since it was smaller than her thumb. She will not speak of it. The party may glimpse it on a Perception DC 17 check, and Aldea's only response to a direct question will be: "You have good eyes. Drink your tea."

What she does in Phase 5

Two things, one already done and one available on request:

  1. The written reply she sent to Trina (forwarded to Caelith) lays out three observations: the wax, the hand, the geographic impossibility. Players have read this in the debrief.
  2. If the party visits her in person (Vector B, see Phase 5 — Leads & Finale), she will receive them politely, take the originals of every recovered marginalia sample, study them in front of the party for one to three hours (depending on what they brought), and answer three to five additional questions slowly and precisely. She will not name the writer. She will not speculate about the writer's species or location.

The right question (GM-only)

Aldea has one private speculation she will not write down: she believes the writer is not human, and learned the surface court hands by reading documents that came up to them rather than by being taught at a desk anywhere on the surface. She will not say this unless asked a specific question in person, in her workshop, with the workshop door closed. The right question is some variant of: "Could the writer have learned this hand from someone who has never been to the surface?" If asked the question that way, she will not lie. She will look at the asker for a long moment and say: "Yes. That is what I think. I do not know who they are. I do not know where they are. I do not know what they are. I will not write any of this down."

If asked the wrong way (e.g., "Is the writer in the Underdark?" or "Is the writer a drow?"), she will turn the question aside gently: "I would not know how to answer that. Ask me what I can read, not what you guess."

Whatever she says in person, she will not write it down. The party who carries away her speculation carries it in their heads.

Voice

Slow, careful, courteous. Uses elven-formal address with strangers ("esteemed traveler," "guest of my friend Trina"), drops the elaboration entirely once she has decided someone is worth her time. Does not joke. Does not flinch. When she does not know an answer, she says "I do not know." When she suspects an answer but cannot prove it, she says "I do not write what I cannot read."

Statblock summary (CR 1/4, PB +2)

AC 12 · HP 22 (5d8) · Speed 30 ft. · Init +1 · Passive Perception 14

STR 10 (+0) · DEX 12 (+1) · CON 11 (+0) · INT 18 (+4) · WIS 16 (+3) · CHA 14 (+2)

Saves: INT +6, WIS +5. Skills: Arcana +6, History +6, Insight +5, Investigation +6, Nature +5, Perception +5.

Senses: Darkvision 60 ft. (half-elf), Passive Perception 14. Languages: Common, Elvish, Old Aerdi, Suloise, two cipher families (Yeomanry diplomatic and a southern Suel trade cipher), reads three additional ancient scripts.

Traits:

  • Fey Ancestry. Advantage on saves vs being charmed; magic cannot put her to sleep.
  • Reader of Hands. Aldea can identify the era, region, and likely paleographic school of any document she has time to study (one hour minimum), and the recipe-family of any sealing wax she can examine in person.

Combat behavior: avoids. If the cottage is attacked she will withdraw into the workshop, bar the door, and use a small bell-rope (which summons her brindle dog, the great horned owl, and — if the threat persists — three guardian creatures the party should not fight unless prepared for a hard tier-2 encounter; treat as Awakened Treant, CR 2 reskinned as a thicket-spirit, plus two Dire Wolves loyal to the valley). She will not initiate.

Councilman Ortwell Brane

The Phase 1 closure-faction figure now elevated. Brane sponsored the Civic Reconciliation Closure motion; it passed 4–3 and is in its mandatory fourteen-day review period when Phase 5 opens. Brane is not corrupt. He is not E.'s asset. The conspiracy's most useful weapon in Loftwick is his honest belief that public order outweighs continuing inquiry. He cannot be bought, threatened, or shamed effectively. He can be outmaneuvered. He should not simply fold; the party's strongest arguments only soften him.

Description

Mid-fifties. Tall, clean-shaven, plain dark Yeomanry councilor's coat with the small bronze wheatsheaf pin of seated office. Iron-grey hair worn short. Hands that have done office work for thirty years and field work before that. Married, four grown children (none in office). Lives in a modest councilor's house on the Civic Way's quieter end. Speaks deliberately. Smiles rarely.

Background

A second-generation Loftwick civic functionary. His father served three terms on the Council in the 1550s; Brane himself has held a seat since 1568. Voted against several wars in his early career. Voted for fewer in his middle career, on grounds that votes against war that one cannot stop are theatre. Considered, in his prime, one of the Council's quieter consensus-builders. Authored two pieces of civic code that are still in routine use, one regulating the Audit Hall's foreign correspondence powers (yes — the same powers his Closure motion now restricts), one governing the militia paymaster's reporting obligations.

What he believes

Brane believes the Yeomanry is small, the Aerdy entanglement that the Vandrell matter has revealed is large, and the Republic cannot afford to be drawn into a multi-year inquiry that will require either Aerdy diplomatic exposure or a Yeomanry militia commitment to the eastern theater that the southern districts cannot spare. He believes Caelith Dunivar is a competent and dedicated Hand of the Duke whose work in this matter has nonetheless taken the Audit Hall further from its Yeomanry mandate than the Audit Hall's mandate permits. He believes that closing the file is the painful but correct civic decision, and that the alternative — continuing — invites a future the Yeomanry has neither the resources nor the appetite to fight.

None of this is a lie. None of it is propaganda. Brane is, in his own framing, the responsible adult in the room.

Why he is dangerous

Because he is right enough. The Yeomanry is small. The Aerdy entanglement is large. The Republic cannot fund a multi-year court action in Aerdi commercial court without rebudgeting; the militia funding question Edda Halvern raised is real; the bandit pressure on the Hool Marsh trade road is real. Brane's strongest argument is that public order has costs, and those costs are paid in the southern districts whether the Council debates them or not. The party's strongest argument is that the conspiracy is using Brane's good intentions as a shield. Both arguments are correct. The party must outmaneuver the conspiracy without making Brane the villain.

What he wants from the party

Nothing. He has no use for them. He will receive them politely if they request an audience, listen to whatever they have to say, ask one or two pointed questions, and decline to change his position based on what they tell him. He is not for sale. He is not for trade. He is, however, willing to listen to a procedural compromise if one is presented through Mairra Voss or Edda Halvern (see their pages).

How the party can succeed against him

Three tactics, none of which destroys him politically (which is not the goal):

  1. Edda Halvern side deal. Address the militia funding question through alternative funds (a Trina contribution, plus a redirected Audit Hall budget line). Halvern withdraws her co-sponsorship; the motion's vote shifts from 4–3 to 3–4. Brane respects the move and does not retaliate. This is the cleanest win.
  2. Public exposure of the conspiracy's use of the motion. If the party can demonstrate at the council session that the conspiracy is actively benefiting from the Closure motion (e.g., by showing Vesten Quill's courier receipt or by exposing the broadside attack as enemy work), Brane will move privately for a procedural recess. The motion does not pass on Day 9; it is held over for further consideration. This is the noisier win.
  3. Mairra Voss procedural alliance. Mairra Voss will support a renewed vote if the party brings her a clean documentary case. The renewed vote requires three days' notice and is more likely to reach 3–4 if the party has also moved Halvern. This is the slowest but most permanent win.

What he will not do

  • Resign.
  • Apologize.
  • Acknowledge that the Closure motion has been useful to the conspiracy unless presented with proof he cannot deny.
  • Move against Caelith personally.
  • Cooperate with the Audit Hall's inquiry on his own initiative.

Statblock summary (CR 1/2, PB +2)

AC 12 · HP 31 (7d8) · Speed 30 ft. · Init +1 · Passive Perception 13

STR 11 (+0) · DEX 12 (+1) · CON 13 (+1) · INT 15 (+2) · WIS 16 (+3) · CHA 16 (+3)

Saves: WIS +5, CHA +5. Skills: History +4, Insight +5, Persuasion +5.

Combat behavior: none. Yields. Will not initiate violence under any circumstances Phase 5 contemplates.

Councilor Edda Halvern

The named co-sponsor of the Closure motion. Senior councilor, sixties, widow, formerly served as militia paymaster for the southern districts. Her motive is unrelated to Brane's. She wants the Audit Hall's foreign-inquiry budget redirected to a militia funding shortfall in the southern districts. She is honest but pragmatic. Useful to E. by accident, not aligned with E. The party can offer her a side deal.

Description

Mid-sixties. Short, square build, iron-grey hair worn under a plain widow's cap with the southern-district pewter pin (the only ornament she wears). Plain dark wool dress, militia-paymaster boots that have outlasted their original use. Speaks plainly. Listens harder than she speaks.

Background

Born in the Hool Marsh district. Married a militia officer who died in a southern bandit engagement in 1571. Took her late husband's paymaster role in 1572 to keep the family income; held it competently for seven years; was elected to her current Council seat in 1580 on the strength of her paymaster record. Has watched the Hool Marsh trade road's security degrade for the past three years and has filed eleven separate funding requests with the Council, all denied for budget reasons.

Why she co-sponsored the Closure motion

Brane approached her three weeks before the motion was filed. He offered her a deal: if Halvern co-sponsored the motion, the Audit Hall's foreign-inquiry budget — currently 8,000 gp annually — would be redirected. 5,000 gp would go to the southern district militia (her stated need), 3,000 gp would be returned to the general civic fund. Halvern agreed. The deal was not corrupt; it was a budget reallocation she had been seeking for two years through legitimate channels. Brane was the first councilor willing to deliver it.

She is not aware that her co-sponsorship benefits a foreign conspiracy. She would care if she knew, but she is not certain she would change her vote — the militia funding shortage is killing carters and farmers on a road she rode as a girl, and the Vandrell matter is, to her ear, a matter for Aerdy commercial courts rather than for Yeomanry public funds.

How the party can move her

Replace the funds without the motion. If the party can secure 5,000 gp for the southern district militia from a non-Audit-Hall source, Halvern will withdraw her co-sponsorship and the motion's vote shifts from 4–3 to 3–4. The replacement funds need not be permanent (a one-year emergency contribution is sufficient to release her vote on this cycle), but the source must be credible.

Three sources are realistic at the party's level:

  1. Trina Alvere. Trina can quietly provide 3,000 gp from her own household; she will do so if Caelith asks and the party has approached her in person.
  2. The party themselves. If the party has accumulated significant gold from Phase 4 (e.g., the strongroom haul on Path C, or the freed Phase 4 escrow on Path A), they can contribute the remainder. Caelith will refuse to take this gold for himself but will accept it for Halvern's stated purpose.
  3. A redirected portion of the Audit Hall's discretionary fund — only available on Path A or Path B, where Caelith retains some institutional cover. Costs Caelith a favor with one Audit Hall contact, who must be repaid in Phase 6.

The deal must be presented to Halvern by Caelith or by Mairra Voss; she will not accept it directly from the party. Halvern is not a friend the party makes. She is a problem the party solves through the right intermediary.

What she will not do

  • Vote against the militia funding question, even if the party offers her larger payments.
  • Take the funds personally. (The funds must go to the southern district militia paymaster's account, with documentation.)
  • Cooperate with any approach that smells of bribery. The deal is a budget reallocation, not a payment to her.

Statblock summary (CR 0, PB +2)

AC 12 · HP 18 (4d8) · Speed 30 ft. · Init +1 · Passive Perception 13

STR 11 (+0) · DEX 12 (+1) · CON 13 (+1) · INT 14 (+2) · WIS 16 (+3) · CHA 12 (+1)

Skills: History +4, Insight +5, Persuasion +3, Survival +5.

Combat behavior: none. (She carried a shortsword as a paymaster; she still keeps it under her bed; she will not draw it in Phase 5.)

Councilor Mairra Voss

The sympathetic councilor — the party's potential ally in opposing Closure. Late fifties, formerly a trade-clearing notary in Loftwick (a parallel professional to Veska Maelan; they have never met). She voted against the Closure motion when it first came to floor and lost 4–3. She will support the party's procedural effort if they bring her a clean documentary case.

Description

Late fifties. Yeomanry, fair-skinned, sandy hair gone mostly grey and worn in a single thick braid down her back. Dark wool councilor's coat over a notary's high-collared linen shirt — she still dresses, in Council, as the trade-clearing notary she was. A small silver scale-and-scroll badge at her throat (her old notarial seal, kept past retirement). Walks with a slight limp from a riding accident in her thirties.

Background

Practiced as a trade-clearing notary in Loftwick from 1554 to 1576 — twenty-two years. Specialized in Aerdy and Keoish commercial chops; her professional library remains the most complete in the Yeomanry outside the Audit Hall itself. Elected to the Council in 1577 on the strength of her notarial reputation. Has voted with the consensus on most matters and against it on a small number where, in her words, "the consensus had not yet read the file carefully."

Where she stands on the Closure motion

She voted against. She lost 4–3. She has not raised the matter again on her own — Council procedure makes a third raised vote of an already-tabled matter difficult, and a third loss would politically end her ability to raise it in this Council. If the party brings her a clean documentary case (i.e., evidence the Closure motion is benefiting a foreign conspiracy that has demonstrably moved against Yeomanry assets), she will procedurally support a renewed vote. The renewed vote requires three days' notice and Mairra's standing as the procedural sponsor.

What she needs from the party

One of the following, in order of strength:

  1. The compromised courier receipt (Handout 6) demonstrating that an active enemy network has been operating against the Audit Hall during the Closure motion's review period. Strongest case.
  2. Vesten Quill's confession if the party has taken him alive. Equally strong but politically louder; Mairra will accept it but will warn the party that the Council will require some explanation of how they obtained it.
  3. The Aldea reply combined with the Greyhawk affidavit. Together these establish that the conspiracy continues to operate beyond Sereth's removal. Weaker procedurally but politically credible.
  4. Caelith's testimony in person. Caelith will offer this if no other path is open; Mairra will accept it; the political cost to Caelith of testifying personally is high (it makes him visibly partisan in a Council vote about his own office).

How she works

Mairra Voss is procedurally meticulous. She will not raise the renewed vote without a documented case. She will not accept a documented case without verifying its provenance. She will not verify a provenance without giving the party at least one chance to retract anything they have provided in error. She is a notary first, a councilor second. Players who treat her as a pure ally will lose her; players who treat her as a procedural specialist will earn her.

Voice

Notarial-formal. Speaks in clean clauses with explicit subordination. When she agrees, she nods once and writes down what she has agreed to. When she disagrees, she says so plainly. When she does not yet know, she says: "I will read it. I will tell you in the morning."

Statblock summary (CR 0, PB +2)

AC 11 · HP 22 (5d8) · Speed 30 ft. · Init +0 · Passive Perception 14

STR 10 (+0) · DEX 11 (+0) · CON 11 (+0) · INT 16 (+3) · WIS 16 (+3) · CHA 14 (+2)

Skills: History +5, Insight +5, Investigation +5, Perception +5, Persuasion +4.

Combat behavior: none. Yields.

pages.name: Marshal Rennic Thale — Loftwick Militia Commander

text.content:
Marshal Rennic Thale

The Loftwick militia commander. Honest, competent, careful with his orders. Council-instructed to "be aware" of the party's movements (specifically on Path C; on Paths A and B he has been left to his own judgment). May be persuadable to support the courier-chain investigation if the party approaches him correctly. Will not act without orders. Will not refuse an order he has been given.

Description

Late forties. Yeomanry, weathered, broad-shouldered, dark-blond hair cropped short with the white scar of an old head wound visible at the hairline above his left ear. Plain Yeomanry militia coat with the marshal's bronze gorget — the gorget is polished but unornamented. Carries a heavy clasp-knife at his belt and a longsword scabbarded across his back when on duty.

Background

A career militia officer. Twenty-two years in service. Commanded the southern detachment during the 1571 Hool Marsh bandit campaign (the campaign in which Edda Halvern's husband died). Promoted to Marshal of the Loftwick city detachment in 1578. Married, two grown sons (one a journeyman blacksmith, one in militia service in the western districts). Lives in a militia house adjacent to the city barracks.

Where he stands

Thale is not political. He follows lawful Council orders. He has been instructed by Brane's faction (with the Council's procedural blessing) to be aware of the party's movements during the Closure motion's review period. On Paths A and B, this instruction is loose — Thale interprets it as "keep an eye on the Little Palace and report unusual activity." On Path C, the instruction is firmer — Thale interprets it as "observe the party in public spaces and report comings and goings to Brane's office daily." In neither case has he been ordered to act.

What he will do for the party

If the party approaches him directly and is honest about the courier-chain investigation, Thale will not refuse to help. He will, however, require:

  1. A reasonable explanation — not the full conspiracy, but enough that he can write a report he could defend to the Council if asked. ("The party is investigating an active enemy courier network operating against the Audit Hall" is enough.)
  2. That his help be quiet. He will not station militia at the dead-drop or at the printer's room. He will, however, brief his night-watch commanders to look the other way for specific named alley-routes during specific hours, and to respond to a specific alarm signal (a militia whistle from a designated rooftop) within three minutes.
  3. That the Council's standing instruction be respected on paper. He will continue filing his "awareness" reports daily; he will simply omit the courier-chain operation from those reports. This is a small but real risk for him.

If the party approaches him with threats, with bribes, or with implications that he is corrupt, he will refuse to help and may report the approach to Brane. He is not for sale, and he is not afraid.

Optional combat support

If the party engages Vesten Quill at the printer's room and the situation escalates beyond two Thugs (e.g., the conspiracy reinforces, or the printer is unexpectedly armed), Thale's whistle response (three militia, including Thale himself, arriving within three minutes) is the party's contingency. Use this only if the party has earned it and only if the encounter genuinely needs the support. Thale's arrival in a fight will subdue rather than kill — the militia carry batons and longswords, and Thale will personally take charge of any prisoners.

Statblock summary (CR 4, PB +2)

AC 18 (chain mail and shield) · HP 65 (10d8+20) · Speed 30 ft. · Init +1 · Passive Perception 13

STR 16 (+3) · DEX 13 (+1) · CON 14 (+2) · INT 12 (+1) · WIS 13 (+1) · CHA 14 (+2)

Saves: STR +5, CON +4. Skills: Athletics +5, Insight +3, Intimidation +4, Perception +3.

Senses: Passive Perception 13. Languages: Common, Old Aerdi.

Actions

  • Multiattack. Two attacks: one longsword + one shield bash (or two longsword if both hands free).
  • Longsword. +5 melee, reach 5 ft. Hit: 1d8+3 slashing (1d10+3 two-handed).
  • Shield Bash. +5 melee, reach 5 ft. Hit: 1d4+3 bludgeoning, target makes a DC 13 STR save or is knocked prone.
  • Heavy Crossbow. +3 ranged, range 100/400. Hit: 1d10+1 piercing.

Reactions

  • Parry. When hit by a melee attack he can see, Thale adds +3 to AC against that attack (potentially turning a hit into a miss).
  • Subdue (1/short rest). When Thale reduces a creature to 0 HP with a melee attack, he may choose to knock them unconscious instead of killing them, with no penalty to the attack.
